title: Regression vs Classification: The Two Supervised Tasks
concept: Supervised learning tasks split into two main types: regression, which predicts a number, and classification, which predicts a category.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In supervised learning, a model trains on data that already has the correct answers attached. What are those correct answers called?
exercises.explain: Right! Labels are the correct answers a supervised model learns from.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In supervised learning, the model must be given labeled examples before it can learn to make predictions.
exercises.explain: True! That's what makes it "supervised" — the correct answers guide the learning process.

cards.title: Two different questions
cards.body: Imagine asking a weather app two different questions: "What temperature will it be tomorrow?" (a number like 78°F) and "Will it rain tomorrow?" (yes or no). These are two totally different kinds of predictions.

cards.title: Two supervised learning tasks
cards.body: Regression is a supervised learning task where the model predicts a number (like a price or temperature). Classification is a supervised learning task where the model predicts a category (like spam or not spam).

cards.title: A dial vs. a set of boxes
cards.body: Regression is like a dial that can land anywhere on a scale — any number, big or small. Classification is like a set of labeled boxes — the answer has to be one of the boxes, nothing in between.

cards.title: House price vs. email spam
cards.body: A house-price tool predicts a number, like $310,500 — that's regression. An email app decides if a message is spam or not spam — that's classification.

cards.title: It's not about which is "harder"
cards.body: It's tempting to think one task type is more advanced than the other. In reality, regression and classification are just two different question shapes — "how much?" versus "which one?" — and picking the right one depends on the problem, not on difficulty.

right.text:
Answers "which one?"
Output: a category
e.g. Spam or Not Spam

exercises.prompt: A fitness app predicts how many calories you burned during a workout. Is this regression or classification?

options.text: Regression, because calories burned is a number
Neither — this isn't something AI can predict

exercises.explain: Right! Calories burned is a number that can be almost anything, so this is regression.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that sorts photos into "cat", "dog", or "bird" is doing regression.
exercises.explain: False! Sorting into a fixed set of labels is classification, not regression.

exercises.sentence: Regression predicts a ___, while classification predicts a category.
exercises.explain: Regression's job is to output a number; classification's job is to output a category.

exercises.prompt: Which one of these outputs comes from a classification model?
exercises.explain: A category label like "High" risk is a classification output — not a number on a continuous scale.

exercises.prompt: Which pair correctly matches task type to output type?
exercises.explain: Exactly! Regression always outputs a number; classification always outputs a category.

exercises.prompt: A hospital wants a tool that estimates how many days a patient will stay after surgery. Should this be built as regression or classification, and why?

options.text: Regression, because length of stay is a number that can take many possible values
Classification, because there are only two outcomes: short or long stay
Regression, because doctors always work with categories
Classification, because number of days is not measurable

exercises.explain: Exactly! Length of stay can be 1 day, 4 days, 12 days, or any other number — that's a job for regression.

exercises.prompt: A streaming service wants to predict whether a user will cancel their subscription this month (yes/no) AND separately wants to predict how many hours they'll watch next week. Which statement is correct?

options.text: The cancellation prediction is classification; the watch-hours prediction is regression
Both predictions are regression because they both involve app usage
Both predictions are classification because both are about user behavior
The cancellation prediction is regression because canceling costs the company money

exercises.explain: Right! Yes/no is a category (classification), while hours watched is a number (regression) — same company, two different task types.
